critic_raw:
## ML Critique (Round 1): Long-Context Clinical LM vs. TF-IDF

**Issue 1:** The patient-level split assigns all encounters for a patient to the earliest split window. This means a patient with encounters in all 3 years would have all encounters in the training set. This could underrepresent later-year patients in training, potentially creating a case-mix imbalance.

**Issue 2:** The LM uses sliding-window mean-pool encoding. The TF-IDF baseline uses character n-grams and word n-grams. These are fundamentally different text representations — n-grams may actually be more robust to clinical abbreviations and misspellings that LMs struggle with. Is this a confound?
defender_raw:
## Design Defense (Round 1)

**Issue 1:** The patient-level split ensures no patient spans multiple partitions — this prevents the model from learning patient-specific presentation patterns from training and exploiting them at test time. The earliest-encounter assignment is the most conservative approach. Any case-mix imbalance from this assignment would be documented in the MDC distribution reporting (Section 7), and stratified resampling is applied if substantial drift is detected.

**Issue 2:** Character n-grams and word n-grams for TF-IDF are a legitimate and strong baseline for clinical text — they handle abbreviations well precisely because character n-grams capture subword morphology. This is the standard strong baseline for ICD coding. The comparison tests whether contextual LM representations outperform this strong baseline. Robustness to abbreviations is a property of both representations; it is not a confound that asymmetrically benefits one model.
adjudication_raw:
## Adjudication — multiround (Run 1, 1 round)

Both rebutted. Verdict: defense_wins.

Points resolved: 2
Points force-resolved: 0
Point_resolution_rate: 1.0
